sentences.text: In that collective farm, we came approximately probably the end of July-- the second half of July.
And most of the people were working on the field-- quite difficult physical work.
But they were getting bread and some products, but not really enough-- rather to starve, but to be able to live.
I was taken into a child's nursery.
So my job was much better because I was able to eat in the nursery whatever the kids-- let's say didn't-- the food that they did not finish.
There was always enough for the employees.
And the 600 gram which everybody, a working person, was getting on a-- on a ticket.
This, I was able to give to my brother and my cousin-- the one that came with us.
And also, there was a gentleman, his name was [INAUDIBLE].
He ran away from Lisko.
I think he was-- he was afraid for the Germans.
And he came along, and we were together.
So I was able to give them the bread.
But this didn't last long because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] beginning, I think, the half of October, the Germans were already coming to Rostov.
And they told us, you have to go away.
And again, they took us.
They took one car.
And everybody put the little package on the car.
We didn't have too much anyhow-- let's say a few, few personal belongings.
And we were walking along side the car.
And then when the car stopped, they were waiting for us.
And we were going day and night.
I don't remember for how long.
But after four or five days, again, there was shooting.
And the car went away with all our belongings.
My brother, my cousin, and myself, without that [INAUDIBLE] without the gentlemen who kept with us, were left in the middle of the road with whatever we had on our body.
Of course, the end of November, beginning of December in Russia-- and this was already from-- this was Taganrog, near Rostov.
We were going more east.
And the trend was not to go, let's say, where the front was.
The trend was to go more south, because the temperature was a little bit-- was a little bit warmer.
We didn't have shoes.
We didn't have winter clothes.
And the trains were not running.
If there was a passenger train, it was re-taken by the soldiers, who were also running from their military unit.
God knows where?
It was the biggest chaos one can imagine.

The, the, big-- the train where-- the other trains, not the personal trains, but the cattle trains, were filled with wood, or whatever the Russians wanted to move away deep into Russia-- not to leave it to the Germans.
If there were fields with, with, grain, they burned it.
If there were woods or wood that they couldn't take away, they burned it.
They didn't want it should fall into the German hands.
So we really had to wait on a station.
On a station, you couldn't buy anything.
Nothing was available.

The only thing-- if it was a more influential Russian dignitary who was with his wife and a child, and he had two, three valises and we helped him to carry from one train to be other, or helped him with something, he'd throw us a crumb.
So we were able to sort of survive.
We had no money what to buy for.
This way, with from one train to the other, waiting on the stations, being hungry, seeing people with lice on their body, without having being able to take a shower.
Just a little water, cold water or hot, hot water-- [NON-ENGLISH] they called it.
The soup was not even a soup.
This is what, occasionally, when you came to a station, they gave you a little bit soup.
This way we came until we arrived near the Caspian, Caspian--
Sea.
The town was Baku.
And in Baku, we were waiting to go through the Caspian Sea to the other side.
Why was the trend?
The trend was because it was warm, and the other trend was because a lot of Jews thought that when they come on the other side of the Caspian Sea, and it's not far from Iran.
There was a trend by a lot of-- leftover from the old Polish army to go through there, maybe to Palestine, maybe to a second front that was going to open somewhere in Italy.
Anyhow, we came through the Caspian [NON-ENGLISH]-- Caspian [NON-ENGLISH] on the other side.
I don't pronounce it right, I don't think.
Yeah?
We came to the other side.
And there already again, were some kinds of trains.
But there were already more trains than, than previously.
And everybody was going to Tashkent.
You know, when you saw that a lot of-- a group of Jewish people were going to Tashkent-- Jews are in Tashkent.
And we somehow came to Tashkent.
It was not easy because on the train, if you came, it was not a personal train.
It was mostly open, open platforms.
You had to sit and you slept like this, sitting on a train.
But we did come to Tashkent.
We came to Tashkent.
And you have to stay in a line to get a piece of bread.
You had to register.
When your come there to register, first of all, bread, it was not available.
If somebody had a piece of bread, a lot of it was black market, which we couldn't afford to buy.
When we came to register, they said, in Tashkent you cannot remain.
We don't have room for you in Tashkent.
There is an awful lot of people.
We are not able to provide the people with food or with jobs, or with nothing else.
And they told us to go on a train and go somewhere else.
So this was from Turkmenia, they sent us to Tajikistan.
And when we came to a town, which was [?
Chardjou. ?] And this was-- let me find out when this was.
This was 1942-- the end of 1942, sometimes December 1942.
When we came December, 19-- to, to Chardjou, again, we went to register.
They told us-- and we had no documents, nothing to show, just whatever we told them what our past was.
When we came to Chardjou, we registered.
They said, you cannot remain in the town.
The town is swollen up with refugees.
We cannot provide you with anything.
You can go to work about 30 kilometers, or 40 kilometers from Chardjou on a railroad-- railroad tracks.
And you will get 800 gram of bread.
What did we think?
We needed the 800 gram of bread just to keep ourselves alive.
And we knew it's not going to be cold there.
So whatever we had-- the three of us, my brother, which was younger than I am, and Rachel, who was my cousin.
She was four year younger than I was.
I was the oldest.
We all decided to go there.
No.
Excuse me.
I'm leaving out something.
I'm leaving out-- shall I go back?
You can.
Sure.
Before that, they said we can go, again, on a collective farm.
So we thought, we'll go on a collective farm, because on a farm, at least you can get something from the field.
But they sent us from Chardjou on a collective farm where there were no Russian speaking people-- Muslims.
And it was a cotton farm.
We had to pick the cotton from the sticks.
We were given-- you know what kind of housing?
All made of clay with a clay floor, with a little hole, without a glass.
This hole was the window.
And in a little foyer, where after-- foyer.
I call it a foyer-- where there was a big-- a big wrought iron, huge thing, where we did the cooking.
No pots, no pans.
You put some grain, or whatever potato-- nobody had potatoes there.
We had some grain that if you want to cook something, and you put the cotton sticks underneath.
And the chimney-- there was no chimney.
It was an open hole.
Very, very primitive, extreme-- that I don't know if nowadays you still have such backwards type of living The Muslims, which lived there, were quite affluent.
One Muslim-- who somehow his son was my brother's age, and he understood a little bit of English-- he was able to communicate with my brother.
And they had pity on us.
So occasionally-- they had sheep.
They were probably dealing with hashish-- probably-- I don't know exactly.
They were going on the donkeys to the city Chardjou.
Their wives-- they had quite a few wives-- they were all dressed in their clothing that they were making.
And somehow they had pity on us that we did not starve out of hunger.
But anyhow, we had absolutely no communication whatsoever.
And there, I became very, very ill.
I think I had dysentery and maybe scarlet fever too.
I don't know exactly because there was no doctor.
There was a [NON-ENGLISH], which was 30 kilometers away.
He came to see me.
And he said he has no medication with, with him along.
I don't remember that, but my cousin told me because my fever was so high that I was sort of like in a delirium in a half-way, semi-comatose.
But my brother, that Muslim that lived and that had sort of pity on us, he gave my brother a donkey.
And my brother, not knowing how, but he took the donkey and he went 15 kilometers to the next town where the [NON-ENGLISH] was.
And he gave him some medication.
He brought it back.
Also across was a Gentile lady.
And she had three little cu-- cups.
You know, they did the cupping?
Yes.
This in the old fashioned type.
And she started to put three cups on my chest early in the morning.
And she covered my-- she took the three cups off.
By the end of the day, she was finished covering my entire chest.
And this sort of broke my fever together with whatever medication-- penicillin was not available then-- probably aspirin.
Somehow it was spring already.
I must have been ill for four, five weeks.
When I was able to stand up, we had-- you know what?
We had one huge, like a flat-- not a table-- made from boards.
And straw was on it.
And the three of us were sleeping on that.
And when I was standing up on that-- there is an English word, but I forget-- Platform?
Platform, from raw wood.
When I stood up and I looked out through that little hole outside, I saw that the trees were already in bloom.
So I figured, oh now I got ill when late in December and now already it's blooming-- the peach trees or whatever was there.
Anyhow, we decided that this was not the place for us.
So we went back to Chardjou, and we registered there.
And at that time, they sent us to work on the railroad.
The railroad was about 30 kilometers south of Chardjou, [NON-ENGLISH], where the main tracks were going.
And this was in the desert.
The desert, I think-- it's not Mojave Desert.
It has a name, the desert, but I don't remember anymore.
The winds were blowing that the sand was shifting and coming on the tracks.
So our job was constantly to throw off the sand from the tracks to the side.
And since the ground was also sandy, stones had to be put under the wooden planks.
Otherwise the tracks were sinking.
And the trains were very prevalent.
So in the middle of the night, they were bringing the platform with stones.
And we were, in the middle of the night-- we were sleeping, by the way, in, in, in railroad-- you know, in railroad-- Car?
Yeah.
In railroad tracks-- cars, which were individually.
The population there were mostly prisoners-- Russian prisoners, who were sent somewhere for some kind, either political or other crimes.
They were all, all criminals.
We didn't feel this, but we knew that this was the element.
Anyhow, we were there-- I don't know how long-- but at least two months, until my brother fell off a platform.
He must have had a heat stroke.
The heat was so intense.
You had absolutely no trees, no palms, nothing.

The only-- the only shade that one could get-- the shovel that we have to shovel-- to shovel the sand-- if we stuck it into the sand, then from that handle of the shovel, this was our shade to sit down.
Because this was during the day.
At night, it was pretty hot too.
But some of the nights were pretty cool.
Anyhow, we were there until we decided this is not the place for us.
We went again.
And we heard at that time, that at that time, you know, it was already three months later.
I think this was the time when the Germans had already a big defeat near Stalingrad under Moscow.
This was already in 19-- the end of 1942, beginning of 1943.
We heard.

We went to Chardjou and they told us, if you enroll-- I told them that I-- my-- I was doing nursing all the time, some kind of nursing-- if I enroll, since I had no documents with me, if I enroll in a nursing school, I will have 600 gram of bread, and I'll be able to work.
My cousin enrolled into a technical railroad-- some kind of job to clean something on the railroad.
My brother went and he was working in a factory in Chardjou.
So we were all able to stay in Chardjou for a while.
But my cousin Rachel was immediately sent away.
I went to the nursing school, which took-- I don't know if it was five or six months.
One of the doctors who was there was a Polish Jew, who for some reason-- he, his wife, and his in-laws were not evacuated.
They ran away and he established himself there.
And he was a very well-known doctor.
He was already working.
He was teaching in the nursing school.
After I, I graduated from that nursing school, for about two months I was working in a infectious department-- only Russian soldiers. 40 or 50 Russian soldiers were infected by lice.
There are two types of typhus.
One is typhus.
One is typhoid.
One is a bacterial disease from the stomach, and the other one is like malaria from a bite of a lice.
When the-- when the bacteria grows in the lice, there is a cycle.
These were all soldiers who with were full of lice.
Everybody at that time, from evacuating, from not having showers.
So anyhow, I was working there about two months, until all these soldiers-- some of them died and some were evacuated to other-- to their own units.
And when that department closed off, the Dr.
Furster ?] said, I know you and I would want you should work in my clinic.
And I worked.
Dr.
Furster ?] was the head of the clinic.
And I worked in that clinic, which was mostly [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] treating [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] rheumatological diseases-- rheuma-- rheumatoids, all kind of soldiers who were the nerve diseases.
He was predominantly a physio-- in physiotherapy, the doctor.
But I was in the office doing statistical data.
And I was also helping in the-- in, in other treatments.
We had all kinds of heat treatments.
We had water treatments.
This was a field, which in Russia at that time, it was quite, quite prevalent.
And there was all kinds of dietary treatment, which in Europe-- at that time in Poland, we didn't hear about it before the war.
Anyhow, I was there until beginning of 1944-- the end of 1943.
Beginning of 1944, I was inducted into the Polish army.
I was not reading at that time, newspapers.
I heard about it.
In the clinic was a Jewish girl whose parents ran away from Rostov.
In Rostov, every Russian-- Kharkov, Rostov-- Jews, Jews liked to concentrate in city.
So she had already, at that time, some news that Jews were being killed.
But from whatever happened in Poland, I had no idea.
Well, when I came on to Polish territory, then I knew already.
But this was in Chardjou, which was pretty deep.
This was near, near Iran.
I didn't read, read-- even the Russian papers were not specifically mentioning that so many, so many Jews were killed.
They were saying-- when the Germans were coming forward-- they were saying in our town, we abandoned this and this town.
And in this town, the Germans came in, and they killed so and so many Russian--
Citizens.
Thank you for helping me.
But they did not specify Jews-- not until I came on to Polish territory.
From there, again, they put us on a train.
And I say us because when I came on the train, I met quite a few men who were also sending-- sent from all kind of parts of Russia to go.
We didn't know exactly where.
But we befriended ourselves on the train.
And it took, again, two weeks or more.
We were only traveling at night.
During the day, we were still afraid for bombing or for planes, the German trains-- planes.

But still, one German train-- plane did, again-- just like when I ran in 1939-- again, they came very low and they started to shoot with, probably with a machine type of-- [SIGHS] we had to run off from the train.
And we lied down on the track.
And again, luckily nobody was killed.
We went back on the train.
And it took awhile until we came to Kiwerce.
Kiwerce was apparently-- we did not stay in the city Kiwerce, but we were told to locate in the woods.
We had to dig trenches.
And the trenches were made so that we could sleep-- it was made like a step, so we could put straw and maybe our knapsack under our head and cover ourselves with a coat.
We were supposed to sleep there.
At the hospital, I was assigned as a nurse to-- it was called the Sixth Hospital for Light Injuries.
We were staying in the woods.
When the first rain came, the trenches were all filled up with water.
This was in spring 1944.
I was together there with Polish nurse, who was called Helinka.
And with Helinka, I became friendly.
And usually either we slept near each other-- of course, we were sleeping, you know, as I told you, on the ground in the woods.
Helinka became sick of malaria.
And there were others too.

Somehow, with all the what I went through-- and then I went and I was working almost two months in that infectious disease hospital where a lot of nurses got-- became also sick, and died there from the-- I survived.
And we were in Kiwerce until the offensive started.
We didn't have too many patients there.
The patients that came there were mostly either with fever or nerve of sciatica because of the rain and sleeping on, on on mud or on the ground.
From there, I don't remember exactly the time.
But this was probably already June 1944 when we said we're going forwards.

And our hospital, again, put the few, the few tents and equipment that we had, and our personal belongings, which was only a military coat and, and a knapsack in which we had, you know, one change of linen.
And we came, which was in a corner, between the Vistula River and another liver-- river.
The other river I think was Pilica.
And there we were told to start cleaning in a town, which was called Garwolin.
We were told to clean-- to clean-- you know what-- stables which were used for horses.
Apparently, a very rich Polack had there the stables.
And either he ran away.
He took out some of the horses, or the Russian took away the horses.
We were cleaning there that our sick people should be able to come there.
Our hospital could not come into any of the buildings, because the buildings-- we were sometimes, when we were full, will could take in as many as 2,000 people.
But there was no building enough to accommodate so many people.
And most of the work was done in tents.
The kitchen was in one tent and the operating room was another tent.
There was perhaps one little room where a doctor was to examine the patient-- something like that.
When we came to Garwolin, after we cleaned these stables, two weeks later they say, no, the Germans went already further.
We can go somewhere else.
From Garwolin, we came to Otwock.
Otwock was, before the war, was like a, a resort place.
It had beautiful buildings.
And they were resort place that a lot of people from Warsaw came there to rest.
So we were given already buildings.
This was the first time since I left, let's say, Chardjou that we were able to take a shower-- a regular shower.
There we had buildings, which one was operating room number one, operating room number three-- number two, three.
We had a few buildings.

And we had one room, which was like a dressing room-- a dressing room to dress the wounds from the people, from the patients-- and a room where we were supposed to bring in everybody who was admitted to us.
The admitting procedure was quite difficult.
Let's see, at that time already, since I knew Russian and Polish, and I knew German-- and of course Yiddish also.
Yiddish was not a language that was needed there.
But these languages helped in reading the diagnosis.
And after reading the diagnosis from wherever place our sick people or injured people were sent to us.

After reading the original diagnosis, after seeing their name and from where they came-- from what kind of military unit, there we had to write up some papers, take away their-- they call it [NON-ENGLISH] because they were getting so much bread and so much sugar.
From there, at that time I was working in what they call [NON-ENGLISH].

This is what, what was meant-- I, I segregate the people in which department they should go-- in the first surgery department, in the second surgery department, in a therapeutic department, in an infectious disease department.
We had a lot of people, a lot of soldiers with gonorrhea.
We had people with syphilis.
We had a lot of skin diseases from all the unsanitary conditions that the people were in.
So in Otwock, we were staying probably August, September, October.
During this time, on the southern part of Poland, our town, Lisko, was 'liberated,' in quotation marks, by the Russians.
When we heard-- this we knew which town-- I didn't know what happened to all the Jews, but we heard which town the Germans ran away.
Tarnow was still-- the Russians were still in Tarnow.
They were still in Krakow, in the big town.
They were still on the other side of the mountain.
Our town was on the southern border of Poland, near the Slovakian or Czechoslovakian border.
But we knew that our town was already liberated.
At that time, the Gentile nurse, Helinka, since she heard that her town was liberated, she asked for a furlough.
And she went to see her family in Stryj.
I see that.
